source: Labour unrest in Malaya- deportation of Chinese communists from Malaya__CO273:662:11
filename: Labour unrest in Malaya- deportation of Chinese communists from Malaya__CO273:662:11.txt
summary:
- File concerns **labour unrest in British Malaya (Straits Settlements/Federated Malay States)** and the related **arrest, internment/banishment, and deportation of suspected Chinese communists**, with attention to wartime security and labour agitation (1940).  
- **Time period:** chiefly **May–December 1940** (with later file handling notes into **Jan 1941**); references include meetings **4 July 1940** and **27 Aug 1940**, and multiple telegrams **Oct–Dec 1940**.  
- **Major action/decision:** Malayan authorities planned to **deport detainees to China via Hong Kong**, with Hong Kong agreeing to accept deportees **in batches for direct transit** to **Sha Yue Chung / Mirs Bay** (unoccupied China), while **not formally handing them over** and generally **not providing nominal rolls** to Chinese authorities.  
- **Dispute over information-sharing:** The **Chinese Ambassador (Chinese Embassy, London)** requested (19 Oct 1940) **full name lists**, **departure dates**, and **ship names**, plus handover to designated Chinese officials; the **Officer Administering the Government, Straits Settlements** replied (14 Nov 1940) that **nominal rolls were unnecessary** and **undesirable to furnish details** to the **Chinese Consul General**—though Colonial Office discussion notes that **international practice** would be to inform the deportees’ government and suggests reconsideration.  
- **Notable numbers:** Chinese Ambassador claimed **“over one thousand”** suspects arrested/interned; Malayan view cited **~610** awaiting deportation, with **315 “political cases”** (and another note: **313 political cases**) versus the Ambassador’s figure; memorandum notes **about 60 already sent** to China.  
- **Key actors/organizations:** British colonial governments in **Straits Settlements (Singapore)**, **Federated Malay States**, and **Hong Kong**; **Colonial Office/Secretary of State for the Colonies**; **Foreign Office**; **Chinese Government (Chungking/National Government)**, **Chinese Ambassador**, and **Chinese Consul General (Singapore)**; **Special Branch/CID police**; **Malayan Communist Party (MCP)** and **Singapore General Labour Union**; **Anti-Enemy Backing-up Society**.  
- **Labour/legislative response:** Federal Council debates and passage of **Trade Unions Bill (Enactment No. 11 of 1940)** and **Industrial Courts Bill (Enactment No. 12 of 1940)** aimed to regulate/legitimize unions and manage disputes; cited communist-organised strikes (e.g., **Batu Arang** 1937 & Dec 1939; **Sungei Besi** Dec 1939 & Mar 1940) and alleged MCP front activity (e.g., **“Workers’ Savings Society”** at **Sungei Lembing**, reported **~130 members**).  
- **Intelligence assessments:** Malaya Combined Intelligence Summaries describe MCP strategy shifts (reviving anti-Japanese platform while reaffirming anti-British aims), increased anti-British/anti-war propaganda, and **uncertain** possible Japanese efforts to turn Chinese sentiment anti-British and to influence labour unrest (claims framed as conjecture/unknown in the reports).
